source_paper_title: Nash Equilibria for Dividend Distribution with Competition
context:
Section 4, Remark 4.9, page 23 (arXiv:2312.07703v3)

The question as to whether an equilibrium exists in which Player 1 uses a strategy and Player 2 uses a control (both either pure or randomised) is open when \(y>x\), especially if we seek an equilibrium valid for all parameter choices and initial states. We leave the question open for future research.
title: Existence of a Nash equilibrium with Player 1 using a strategy and Player 2 using a control in the asymmetric-endowment game
background_latex:
- Two players (firms) have cash reserves \((X_t)_{t\ge 0}\) and \((Y_t)_{t\ge 0}\) driven by a common Brownian motion \((B_t)_{t\ge 0}\) with drift \(\mu_0>0\) and volatility \(\sigma>0\).
- Initial endowments satisfy \(0\le x<y\) and, under dividend controls \(L\) (Player 1) and \(D\) (Player 2), the controlled reserves are
\[X_t^{L}=x+\mu_0 t+\sigma B_t-L_t,\qquad Y_t^{D}=y+\mu_0 t+\sigma B_t-D_t.\]
- Admissible controls are right-continuous, nondecreasing, adapted processes \(L,D\) with \(L_{0-}=D_{0-}=0\) and jump constraints
\[\Delta L_t\le (X^{L}_{t-})_+,\qquad \Delta D_t\le (Y^{D}_{t-})_+,\qquad t\ge 0.
\]
- Default times are \(\gamma_X=\inf\{t\ge 0: X_t^{L}\le 0\}\) and \(\gamma_Y=\inf\{t\ge 0: Y_t^{D}\le 0\}\).
- If one firm defaults first, the survivor becomes a monopolist with drift \(\hat\mu>\mu_0\). Let \(\hat v:[0,\infty)\to[0,\infty)\) be the value function of the classical De Finetti dividend problem with drift \(\hat\mu\).
- Payoffs (discount rate \(r>0\)) are
\[J^1_{x,y}(L,D)=\mathbb E_{x,y}\Big[\int_{[0,\gamma_X\wedge\gamma_Y]}e^{-rt}\,dL_t+\mathbf 1_{\{\gamma_Y<\gamma_X\}}e^{-r\gamma_Y}\,\hat v(X^{L}_{\gamma_Y})\Big],\]
\[J^2_{x,y}(D,L)=\mathbb E_{x,y}\Big[\int_{[0,\gamma_X\wedge\gamma_Y]}e^{-rt}\,dD_t+\mathbf 1_{\{\gamma_X<\gamma_Y\}}e^{-r\gamma_X}\,\hat v(Y^{D}_{\gamma_X})\Big].\]
- A (pure) feedback strategy for Player \(i\) with initial reserve \(x_i\) is a non-anticipative map \(\Psi:[0,\infty)\times C_0([0,\infty))\times D^+_0([0,\infty))\to[0,\infty)\) producing a control path as a functional of the Brownian path and the opponent’s cumulative dividends (Section 3, Definition 3.1, specialized to pure strategies). A (pure) control is a special case that depends only on \((t,B)\) (Definition 3.2).
- A pair of actions is a Nash equilibrium if neither player can improve her payoff by unilateral deviation within the admissible class (Definition 3.6), with deviations allowed in the full class of randomised strategies; in particular, equilibria may involve one player using a strategy and the other using a control.
problem_definition_latex:
Problem: For the game defined above with asymmetric initial endowments \(0\le x<y\), determine whether there exists a Nash equilibrium \((\Psi_1,\Phi_2)\) (in the sense of Definition 3.6) such that:
- Player 1 uses a (pure or randomised) strategy \(\Psi_1\) that is allowed to depend on the opponent’s observed cumulative dividends, and
- Player 2 uses a (pure or randomised) control \(\Phi_2\) that depends only on the Brownian path (i.e., not on Player 1’s actions),
and the equilibrium is well-defined (i.e., induces admissible controls) and valid for all parameter choices \((\mu_0,\hat\mu,\sigma,r)\) and all initial states \((x,y)\) with \(y>x\).
latex:
- Two players (firms) have cash reserves \((X_t)_{t\ge 0}\) and \((Y_t)_{t\ge 0}\) driven by a common Brownian motion \((B_t)_{t\ge 0}\) with drift \(\mu_0>0\) and volatility \(\sigma>0\).
- Initial endowments satisfy \(0\le x<y\) and, under dividend controls \(L\) (Player 1) and \(D\) (Player 2), the controlled reserves are
\[X_t^{L}=x+\mu_0 t+\sigma B_t-L_t,\qquad Y_t^{D}=y+\mu_0 t+\sigma B_t-D_t.\]
- Admissible controls are right-continuous, nondecreasing, adapted processes \(L,D\) with \(L_{0-}=D_{0-}=0\) and jump constraints
\[\Delta L_t\le (X^{L}_{t-})_+,\qquad \Delta D_t\le (Y^{D}_{t-})_+,\qquad t\ge 0.
\]
- Default times are \(\gamma_X=\inf\{t\ge 0: X_t^{L}\le 0\}\) and \(\gamma_Y=\inf\{t\ge 0: Y_t^{D}\le 0\}\).

\[J^1_{x,y}(L,D)=\mathbb E_{x,y}\Big[\int_{[0,\gamma_X\wedge\gamma_Y]}e^{-rt}\,dL_t+\mathbf 1_{\{\gamma_Y<\gamma_X\}}e^{-r\gamma_Y}\,\hat v(X^{L}_{\gamma_Y})\Big],\]
\[J^2_{x,y}(D,L)=\mathbb E_{x,y}\Big[\int_{[0,\gamma_X\wedge\gamma_Y]}e^{-rt}\,dD_t+\mathbf 1_{\{\gamma_X<\gamma_Y\}}e^{-r\gamma_X}\,\hat v(Y^{D}_{\gamma_X})\Big].\]

A (pure) control is a special case that depends only on \((t,B)\) (Definition 3.2).

Problem:
Problem: For the game defined above with asymmetric initial endowments \(0\le x<y\), determine whether there exists a Nash equilibrium \((\Psi_1,\Phi_2)\) (in the sense of Definition 3.6) such that:
- Player 1 uses a (pure or randomised) strategy \(\Psi_1\) that is allowed to depend on the opponent’s observed cumulative dividends, and
- Player 2 uses a (pure or randomised) control \(\Phi_2\) that depends only on the Brownian path (i.e., not on Player 1’s actions),
and the equilibrium is well-defined (i.e., induces admissible controls) and valid for all parameter choices \((\mu_0,\hat\mu,\sigma,r)\) and all initial states \((x,y)\) with \(y>x\).

We leave the question open for future research.
subject_classification: Stochastic Games and Singular Stochastic Control

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
